title: Atomic bombing of Hiroshima
summary: At 08:15 on the morning of 6 August 1945 a single B-29 bomber, the Enola Gay, dropped a uranium fission weapon on the Japanese city of Hiroshima. It detonated some 580 metres above the city with a yield of about fifteen kilotons of TNT. Roughly 70,000 people died within seconds and tens of thousands more in the weeks and months that followed. It was the first use of an atomic weapon in war. Three days later a second bomb devastated Nagasaki, and on 15 August Emperor Hirohito announced Japan's acceptance of the Allied surrender terms.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Hiroshima bomb was the product of the largest research and industrial project of the Second World War. Reports of German experiments in nuclear fission after 1938, and a warning letter drafted by the physicist Leo Szilard and signed by Albert Einstein in August 1939, persuaded President Franklin D. Roosevelt to fund exploratory work. By 1942, under the direction of the US Army Corps of Engineers' Manhattan District, a full-scale programme had been launched with the ambition of building a weapon before Nazi Germany could. Directed by Brigadier General Leslie Groves, with scientific leadership from J. Robert Oppenheimer at Los Alamos in New Mexico, the Manhattan Project employed some 130,000 people at sites including Oak Ridge in Tennessee, Hanford in Washington state, and Chicago's Metallurgical Laboratory, at a cost of about two billion 1940s dollars.

Germany surrendered in May 1945 without an atomic weapon of its own. Japan, however, fought on. The Pacific war had become a campaign of enormous cost: the battles of Iwo Jima (February-March 1945) and Okinawa (April-June 1945) produced more than 100,000 American casualties, and the firebombing of sixty-seven Japanese cities by the XXI Bomber Command under General Curtis LeMay had killed several hundred thousand civilians and destroyed much of Tokyo, Osaka, Nagoya, and Yokohama. An invasion of the Japanese home islands, Operation Downfall, was scheduled for late 1945 and 1946 and expected to cost enormous American and Japanese casualties. Japan's civilian leadership had begun secret peace feelers through Moscow, but its army leadership rejected unconditional surrender and prepared to defend Kyushu.

On 16 July 1945, at the Trinity site near Alamogordo in the New Mexico desert, the first nuclear device (a plutonium implosion weapon codenamed 'the gadget') was successfully tested with a yield of around twenty-one kilotons of TNT. President Harry Truman, who had inherited the presidency on Roosevelt's death in April, received word at the Potsdam Conference. On 26 July the United States, Britain, and Nationalist China issued the Potsdam Declaration demanding Japanese unconditional surrender under threat of 'prompt and utter destruction'. The Japanese government's response, the word mokusatsu, was interpreted in Washington as rejection. On 25 July Truman had already authorised the use of atomic bombs on Japan when they became available. A target committee had short-listed cities that had been left largely untouched by conventional bombing, so that the effects of the new weapon could be studied: Hiroshima, Kokura, Niigata, and Nagasaki. Kyoto, at Secretary of War Henry Stimson's insistence, was removed.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: Mushroom cloud over Hiroshima from an accompanying B-29, 6 August 1945.
image.credit: George R. Caron / US Army Air Forces, NARA, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.body:
Hiroshima in August 1945 was a city of approximately 350,000 people, home of the headquarters of the Second General Army and a major military port; some 40,000 soldiers were stationed there. Around it Japanese schoolchildren had been mobilised to demolish wooden buildings to create firebreaks against the conventional bombing that had, so far, largely spared the city. In the early hours of 6 August, from the Pacific island of Tinian, three B-29 Superfortresses of the 509th Composite Group took off: the Enola Gay, piloted by Colonel Paul Tibbets and carrying the uranium gun-type weapon codenamed Little Boy; and two accompanying aircraft carrying scientific instruments and cameras. An earlier weather-reconnaissance flight had reported clear skies over Hiroshima.

At 07:09 the air-raid sirens sounded for the weather flight and were quickly dismissed. When Tibbets's aircraft approached at 08:14, its lone contrails suggested only reconnaissance and no alarm was given. Little Boy was released from an altitude of about 9,500 metres and detonated 43 seconds later, at 08:15:17 local time, some 580 metres above the courtyard of the Shima Surgical Clinic near the T-shaped Aioi Bridge in the centre of the city. The explosion released roughly 63 terajoules of energy in a fraction of a second: an initial burst of gamma and neutron radiation, a blinding thermal flash reaching several thousand degrees Celsius at the hypocentre, and a supersonic blast wave that flattened almost everything within a radius of one and a half kilometres. A firestorm developed within twenty minutes and burned for hours. The mushroom cloud rose to a reported eighteen kilometres.

Estimates of immediate deaths vary between about 60,000 and 80,000; total deaths through the end of 1945, including those from thermal and blast injuries, acute radiation sickness, and fires, are usually put at around 140,000. Among the dead were thousands of Korean forced labourers, a small number of American prisoners of war, and the schoolchildren mobilised on demolition. The wooden and paper city was reduced to a plain of ash and twisted iron around a few reinforced-concrete shells; the framework of the Prefectural Industrial Promotion Hall, later known as the Atomic Bomb Dome, still stood near the hypocentre. Truman's public statement, released while he was aboard the cruiser Augusta returning from Potsdam, announced that an atomic bomb had been dropped on 'an important Japanese Army base' and warned that a 'rain of ruin' would follow unless Japan accepted the Potsdam terms. On 9 August a plutonium implosion weapon codenamed Fat Man was dropped on Nagasaki by the B-29 Bockscar, killing an estimated 40,000 people immediately and perhaps 70,000 by year's end. The same day the Soviet Union declared war on Japan and Red Army forces invaded Japanese-held Manchuria.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences

image.caption: The ruins of Hiroshima after the bombing, with the shell of the Prefectural Industrial Promotion Hall (Atomic Bomb Dome) visible.
image.credit: US Army Corps of Engineers, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.body:
In Tokyo the two bombings and the Soviet entry into the war overlapped inside a single week of crisis. The Japanese Supreme War Council was split three-three on whether to accept the Potsdam terms; Emperor Hirohito, in an unprecedented intervention on 10 August, told the council he wished the war to end. Attempts by militant officers to seize the imperial palace and destroy the emperor's recorded surrender speech failed. On 15 August, in the first broadcast of his voice, Hirohito told his subjects that Japan would 'endure the unendurable' and accept the Allied terms. The formal instrument of surrender was signed aboard the USS Missouri in Tokyo Bay on 2 September 1945, ending the Second World War.

The medical and social consequences of the two bombings unfolded over decades. By 1950 the Japanese government estimated that some 200,000 people in Hiroshima and 140,000 in Nagasaki had died from causes attributable to the bombings, including radiation-related cancers. The Atomic Bomb Casualty Commission, established in 1947 and later the Radiation Effects Research Foundation, followed survivors, the hibakusha, for the rest of their lives, generating some of the most important epidemiological data on radiation exposure ever produced. Discrimination against hibakusha in marriage and employment was widespread inside Japan for decades.

Strategically the bombs did not end the debate that immediately opened around them. Their proponents, including President Truman and Secretary Stimson in memoirs published soon after the war, argued that they had shortened the war and saved many lives, American and Japanese, that a conventional invasion would have cost. Critics, including some of the Manhattan Project's own scientists, contemporary military figures such as Admirals William Leahy and Chester Nimitz, and later revisionist historians such as Gar Alperovitz, argued that Japan was already close to surrender in the summer of 1945, especially in the face of Soviet entry into the war, and that the bombs had a diplomatic function directed at Moscow as much as a military one against Tokyo. Mainstream scholarship treats both military and diplomatic considerations as real, and the debate remains alive.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Hiroshima and Nagasaki are the only cities to have been attacked with nuclear weapons in war. The images and testimonies produced by their survivors, John Hersey's 1946 New Yorker report Hiroshima, the memoirs of doctors such as Michihiko Hachiya and Takashi Nagai, and the drawings and speeches of the hibakusha, have made them the moral touchstone of the nuclear age. The peace parks laid out at both hypocentres and the annual ceremonies on 6 and 9 August draw hundreds of thousands of visitors and international dignitaries; the ringing of the Peace Bell at 08:15 in Hiroshima has become one of the most watched acts of public remembrance in the world.

The bombings inaugurated a new class of weapons and a new political vocabulary. The Cold War arms race quickly produced thermonuclear devices thousands of times more powerful than Little Boy; by the early 1980s the superpower stockpiles reached about 60,000 warheads. Efforts to control them produced the Baruch Plan (1946), the Partial Test Ban Treaty (1963), the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (1968), and, after the Cold War, the START and New START agreements. Nine states now possess nuclear weapons; the risk of their further spread and use remains the darkest single overhang of contemporary international politics.

Inside Japan Hiroshima helped shape a postwar political identity built around pacifism, Article 9 of the 1947 constitution renouncing war, and a diplomacy that has consistently pressed for nuclear disarmament. Yet Japan also sits under the American nuclear umbrella and its politics of memory around the war remain contested at home and abroad. In museums from the Hiroshima Peace Memorial Museum to the Smithsonian in Washington (where a 1995 exhibition on the Enola Gay produced a bitter public dispute), the question of how to represent the events of 6 August 1945 has itself become a chapter of the story.

worldContext.text: Europe in August 1945 was three months into peace and preparing to divide. The Potsdam Conference at Cecilienhof palace in the Berlin suburbs (17 July to 2 August 1945) had brought together Harry Truman, Joseph Stalin, and Winston Churchill (replaced during the meeting on 28 July by the newly elected Labour prime minister Clement Attlee, whose party had won the British general election of 5 July in a landslide of 393 seats). The Potsdam Declaration of 26 July demanded Japanese unconditional surrender; the concluding communique of 2 August confirmed the reordering of central Europe: the four-power occupation zones of Germany and of Berlin, an Allied Control Council, war-crimes tribunals (Nuremberg would open on 20 November), the transfer of ethnic Germans from Poland, Czechoslovakia, and Hungary in what became forced expulsions of some twelve million people, Polish acquisition of German territories east of the Oder-Neisse line, and Soviet retention of the Baltic states and Konigsberg (renamed Kaliningrad). Britain, France, and much of the western Continent were beginning UNRRA food-relief operations against widespread hunger; George Marshall's aid programme lay two years ahead. In Italy, the Rome CLN government under Ferruccio Parri prepared elections; in France, Charles de Gaulle's provisional government held together an uneasy Communist-Socialist-MRP coalition.

image.caption: Delegates at the Potsdam Conference, 19 July 1945. Truman, Attlee, and Stalin met at Cecilienhof to redraw postwar Europe and issue the surrender ultimatum to Japan.

worldContext.text: British India in August 1945 was in political ferment. The wartime coalition in London had fallen and the incoming Labour government of Clement Attlee was known to favour early decolonisation; Cabinet Ministers would arrive in India within a year. Lord Wavell as viceroy was preparing to convene the Simla Conference of Congress and Muslim League leaders in the autumn (its June 1945 predecessor having failed over Muslim League refusal to accept non-League Muslim nominees). Jawaharlal Nehru, Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel, Maulana Azad, and other Congress leaders had been released from Ahmadnagar Fort and other jails in June after imprisonment since the 1942 Quit India movement. The Bengal famine of 1943, in which some three million people had died, was still a bitter memory, and its causes (wartime rice hoarding, British procurement policies, denial of Burmese imports) were the subject of angry parliamentary questions. Muhammad Ali Jinnah's Muslim League, having swept the 1945 to 46 Muslim reserved seats after its 1940 Lahore Resolution declaration for Pakistan, was pressing hard for a sovereign Muslim state. The Indian National Army trials at the Red Fort of Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose's officers would begin in November, provoking mass demonstrations.

image.caption: Jawaharlal Nehru, first Prime Minister of independent India, in Washington in October 1949; four years earlier, released from British detention in June 1945, he had led Congress into the negotiations that produced partition and independence in August 1947.
image.credit: Abbie Rowe / US National Archives, via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: The Middle East in August 1945 was being reshaped by the end of the war. British and Free French troops had withdrawn from Syria and Lebanon under nationalist pressure after the 29 May 1945 French bombardment of Damascus provoked international protest; both states became fully independent by 1946. The Arab League had been founded in Cairo on 22 March 1945 with Egypt, Iraq, Transjordan, Lebanon, Syria, Saudi Arabia, and Yemen as founding members. In Mandate Palestine, the arrival of Jewish displaced persons from the liberated European camps (survivors of Auschwitz, Bergen-Belsen, and Buchenwald), the November 1945 formation of the underground armed Zionist coalition (Haganah, Irgun, Lehi), and the Anglo-American Committee of Inquiry set the mandate on the road to the November 1947 UN partition vote and the 1948 war. In Egypt King Farouk faced Muslim Brotherhood pressure; in Iran, Soviet troops were still occupying the northern provinces under the wartime Anglo-Soviet Treaty and would soon back the short-lived Azerbaijan People's Government of Ja'far Pishevari and Republic of Mahabad under Qazi Muhammad in 1946, an early Cold War flashpoint. In Iraq, Nuri al-Said manoeuvred in an unstable Hashemite monarchy.

image.caption: Jewish children survivors leaving Buchenwald concentration camp by train for Palestine on 5 June 1945, holding a homemade Zionist flag from the carriage window. US Holocaust Memorial Museum.
image.credit: James E. Myers / US Holocaust Memorial Museum, via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: Africa in August 1945 was hosting large numbers of returning soldiers, some 375,000 African servicemen having fought in the King's African Rifles, the Royal West African Frontier Force, and Tirailleurs Senegalais on fronts from Ethiopia to Burma; their return home fed a growing anticolonial movement. The Fifth Pan-African Congress at Chorlton Town Hall in Manchester (15 to 21 October 1945) would bring together Kwame Nkrumah, Jomo Kenyatta, George Padmore, W.E.B. Du Bois, and Amy Ashwood Garvey in a decisive step towards African independence movements. Ethiopia's Emperor Haile Selassie I, restored to Addis Ababa in May 1941 after the British East Africa campaign expelled Italian occupiers, was moving to modernise his administration and to reclaim Eritrea. In French West Africa, Leopold Sedar Senghor was elected in October 1945 to the French Constituent Assembly for Senegal-Mauritania; Felix Houphouet-Boigny for the Ivory Coast. South Africa under Prime Minister Jan Smuts had helped draft the UN Charter at San Francisco (June 1945) but at home was preparing the ground for the 1948 National Party victory that would inaugurate apartheid under D. F. Malan. In Kenya, Uganda, and Tanganyika, colonial administrations returned to peacetime routines but faced rising African political associations.

image.caption: Emperor Haile Selassie I of Ethiopia in imperial dress; restored to his throne in 1941 after the British expelled the Italian occupiers, he was in 1945 one of the world's most prominent independent African rulers.
image.credit: Ethiopian government official portrait, via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: Australia and New Zealand were coming out of a war that had touched their coasts for the first time; Darwin had been bombed by Japanese aircraft in February 1942 and Japanese midget submarines had penetrated Sydney Harbour in May 1942. Australia had suffered nearly forty thousand military dead (out of around one million servicemen and women mobilised from a population of seven million) and would soon face the enormous challenge of demobilising more than half a million service personnel. The Chifley Labor government (Ben Chifley had succeeded John Curtin, who died in office on 5 July 1945) moved rapidly to expand universal welfare, the Commonwealth Bank, the Snowy Mountains hydro scheme, and by year's end had helped launch the massive Populate or Perish postwar immigration programme under Arthur Calwell that brought in about two million people over two decades. In New Zealand under Peter Fraser's Labour government, similar postwar expansion followed. In the Pacific islands, war damage and prolonged occupation had unsettled prewar colonial orders across Papua and New Guinea (an Australian mandate), the British and French Solomon Islands, and Micronesia, where the American navy had taken the Japanese League of Nations mandate islands and would in 1947 establish the UN Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands.

image.caption: The British light cruiser HMS Belfast at anchor in Sydney Harbour in August 1945, near the Harbour Bridge. Imperial War Museums, ABS 694.
image.credit: Royal Navy official photographer, via Imperial War Museums / Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: In China, the surrender of Japan produced an urgent scramble for territory between Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalists (Kuomintang), whose armies had borne the brunt of the fourteen-year war since the Marco Polo Bridge incident of July 1937 (some fifteen million Chinese had died), and Mao Zedong's Communists (CCP), who had built up bases behind Japanese lines in Shaanxi, Shandong, and Manchuria throughout the war. In August-October 1945 Mao and Chiang met at Chongqing under American mediation by General Patrick Hurley, agreeing on a joint programme that neither trusted. American forces under General Albert Wedemeyer airlifted Nationalist troops to key cities including Beijing and Shanghai; Soviet forces (which had entered the war on 9 August, the day of the Nagasaki bombing) occupied Manchuria and Inner Mongolia, systematically stripped Japanese industrial equipment, and handed captured Kwantung Army weapons to Lin Biao's Chinese Communist forces. Civil war resumed in earnest by 1946 and would end with the Communist proclamation of the People's Republic on 1 October 1949. In Korea, occupied by Japan since annexation in 1910, Soviet forces entered the north from Manchuria while American troops under Lieutenant General John Hodge arrived in Incheon on 8 September 1945, dividing the peninsula at the 38th parallel proposed hastily by two US colonels (one of them Dean Rusk) and setting the stage for the 1950 to 53 Korean War.

image.caption: Mao Zedong and Chiang Kai-shek toasting Japan's defeat at Chongqing in September 1945; within a year they would be at open civil war.
image.credit: Unknown photographer (Bettmann Archive), via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Harry S. Truman
figures.role: US president who authorised the use of atomic weapons against Japan

figures.name: J. Robert Oppenheimer
figures.role: Scientific director of the Los Alamos Laboratory and of the Manhattan Project's weapons work
figures.credit: US Department of Energy / Public domain

figures.name: Leslie R. Groves
figures.role: US Army Corps of Engineers general in overall command of the Manhattan Project

figures.name: Paul W. Tibbets
figures.role: Colonel and pilot of the B-29 Enola Gay that dropped the Hiroshima bomb

figures.name: Emperor Hirohito
figures.role: Emperor of Japan who intervened on 10 August 1945 to accept the Potsdam terms of surrender

figures.name: Kantaro Suzuki
figures.role: Prime minister of Japan in August 1945 who presided over the surrender debate

images.caption: Mushroom cloud over Hiroshima photographed from an accompanying aircraft on 6 August 1945.
images.credit: George R. Caron / US Army Air Forces, National Archives (NARA 542192), via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Ruins of Hiroshima after the atomic bombing, with the Prefectural Industrial Promotion Hall (later Atomic Bomb Dome) visible.
images.credit: US Army Corps of Engineers, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: The B-29 Enola Gay back at Tinian on 6 August 1945 after the Hiroshima mission, with Colonel Paul Tibbets waving.
images.credit: US Army Air Forces, via Wikimedia Commons
